summary: Asparouhova reflects on her complicated relationship with silence around experiences of harassment and abuse of power in tech and other industries. She examines why she, like many women, has kept quiet—normalizing trauma, fearing self-blame, and wanting to define herself by her work rather than victimization. Reading Ronan Farrow's nuanced New Yorker piece on Weinstein finally resonated because it captured the messy psychological reality rather than a simple good-vs-evil narrative. She concludes that true power will come not from public confession or continued silence, but from emotional indifference—no longer flinching at perpetrators' names or avoiding spaces where they might be.
key_points: Binary media portrayals of harassment flatten complex experiences into good-vs-evil narratives that feel reductive and fuel unhelpful public outrage cycles
Victims often normalize trauma by maintaining relationships with perpetrators, blaming themselves, and telling themselves it wasn't that bad
Silence feels protective but actually bonds victims more tightly to their abusers—it's an active choice that requires constant maintenance
Women report as groups not just for stronger evidence but because it keeps the spotlight on perpetrators rather than putting victims' identities on the chopping block
True recovery means reaching emotional indifference—not caring anymore when you see their name or face
money_quotes: When bad things happen to you, you do all the things that these women described in the New Yorker piece. You tell yourself it wasn't so bad, or maybe it was your fault, or you're blowing things out of proportion.
I used to think that not feeling black-and-white about my experiences meant that it must have been my fault, too.

That fuzziness is not because it's unclear whether 'something really did happen' or not, but rather an expected reaction to trauma, in which you try to normalize the behavior and empathize with the other person in order to make sense of the very nonsensical, out-of-control things that have happened to you.
I want to believe that by keeping silent, I'm making the stories go away and living my life as I want. But silence is an active choice, too.
Being this quiet, and working this hard to make sure the stories don't get out, means I am only bonding myself even more irrevocably to these people. Love and hate are equal and opposing forces.
I'd rather define myself by what I do than what's been done to me.
This isn't a story about sex, assault, or even harassment. It's the story that accompanies every abuse of power.
key_insight: Silence around trauma feels like self-protection but actually maintains the bond to perpetrators; true freedom comes not from public confession or continued secrecy, but from reaching genuine emotional indifference.
